tone_system.tone_count_standard.note: Standard Vietnamese has six tones (ngang, sắc, huyền, hỏi, ngã, nặng); modern linguists count 6 phonemic tones — Wikipedia: Vietnamese phonology

tone_system.tones_are_phonemic.note: Vietnamese is a tonal language, so the meaning of each word depends on the pitch in which it is pronounced — Wikipedia: Vietnamese language

tone_system.marked_directly_on_vowel.note: the first tone is not marked and the other five are indicated by diacritics applied to the vowel part of the syllable — Wikipedia: Vietnamese phonology

grapheme_to_phoneme.orthography_mostly_p.note: Chữ quốc ngữ was created by Portuguese missionaries according to the phonemic orthography of Portuguese language — Wikipedia: Vietnamese alphabet

grapheme_to_phoneme.same_sound_multiple_.note: The velar stop is written variously as 'c', 'k', or 'q' in Vietnamese — Wikipedia: Vietnamese phonology / Talk:Vietnamese phonology

phoneme_inventory.is_monosyllabic_analyt.note: Vietnamese is an isolating language (highly analytic)...Vietnamese morphemes and phonological words are predominantly monosyllabic — Wikipedia: Vietnamese language / Vietnamese morphology

syllable_structure.each_syllable_written.note: Vietnamese orthography uses spaces to separate syllables rather than words — Wikipedia: Vietnamese language

word_segmentation.space_marks_syllable_n.note: Vietnamese orthography uses spaces to separate syllables rather than words, meaning multisyllabic words frequently appear as separate tokens — Wikipedia: Vietnamese language

word_segmentation.compound_words_common.note: many multisyllabic words do occur, usually as a result of compounding and reduplication — Wikipedia: Vietnamese morphology

word_order.basic.note: Vietnamese has a basic subject-verb-object (SVO) word order, though utterances may be restructured to be topic-prominent — Wikipedia: Vietnamese grammar

word_order.adjective_after_noun.note: postpositive adjectives are the norm: it is normal for an attributive adjective to follow, rather than precede, the noun it modifies — Wikipedia: Postpositive adjective / Vietnamese grammar

classifiers.required_before_countable_no.note: classifiers are frequently required when a noun is accompanied by a numeral; in Vietnamese the classifier always precedes the noun but comes after the number — Wikipedia: Classifier (linguistics)

classifiers.example_con_for_animals.note: The classifier 'con' generally introduces animate objects, especially animals, e.g. 'con gà' (chicken), 'con chó' (dog) — Wikipedia: Vietnamese grammar

inflection_morphology.no_verb_conjugatio.note: Vietnamese lacks morphological markings of case, gender, number, and tense (and, as a result, has no finite/nonfinite distinction) — Wikipedia: Vietnamese morphology

inflection_morphology.tense_marked_by_pa.note: whereas European languages tend to use morphology to express tense, Vietnamese uses grammatical particles or syntactic constructions — Wikipedia: Vietnamese grammar

pronoun_system.kinship_terms_used_as_pro.note: a pronoun usually connotes a degree of family relationship or kinship, and in polite speech...kinship terminology is used when referring to oneself, the audience, or a third party — Wikipedia: Pronouns in Vietnamese

pronoun_system.choice_depends_on_relativ.note: Vietnamese pronouns indicate the social status between speakers and other persons in the discourse in addition to grammatical person and number — Wikipedia: Pronouns in Vietnamese

sentence_final_particles.exist_in_vietna.note: Languages that use many modal particles in their spoken form include Vietnamese — Wikipedia: Sentence-final particle / Modal particle

politeness_particle_da.dạ_vâng_signal_re.note: 'dạ' combines with 'vâng' to form 'dạ vâng,' a polite affirmative response, particularly used when addressing elders or in formal/respectful contexts — Wikipedia: Vietnamese grammar / Pronouns in Vietnamese

register_taxonomy.mainly_pronoun_and_par.note: Tôi is often used formally...conveys equal status; tao, mày...commonly used in familiar social contexts...otherwise considered impolite — Wikipedia: Vietnamese language / Pronouns in Vietnamese

forms_of_address.title_or_kinship_before.note: the last syllable in a primary name with a title before it is used to call or address a person: 'ông Dũng', 'anh Dũng' — Wikipedia: Vietnamese name

answer_acceptance_vi.diacritic_stripped_.note: Vietnamese can be typed without tone marks, which Vietnamese speakers can usually guess depending on context — Wikipedia: Vietnamese language and computers

naturalness_translation.pronoun_choice_a.note: the exclusive use of the Vietnamese words for 'I' and 'you' are considered informal and rude — Wikipedia: Pronouns in Vietnamese
